title: The CIA’s First Failed Coup
gist: A review of Stephen Long's revisionist history argues the CIA/MI6 Albania operation was never really about toppling Hoxha, that Kim Philby likely played no role in its failure, and that its true cause—Western hubris—is a live lesson for 2026 regime-change ambitions in Iran.

insights.claim: Stephen Long's 'A Rich Harvest of Bitter Fruit' argues the early-Cold-War Albania missions were never necessarily about toppling Enver Hoxha but about assessing how brittle his regime truly was.
insights.speaker: Casey Michel
insights.so_what: Recasts a foundational covert-action myth—relevant to how intelligence failures get narrated.

insights.claim: Long concludes that double agent Kim Philby and the Soviet Union may have played no role whatsoever in the operation's failure, since Albania wasn't worth risking Moscow's top mole.
insights.speaker: Casey Michel
insights.so_what: Debunks the convenient 'betrayal' explanation, reframing failure as self-inflicted.

insights.claim: The six-year operation, which ended in 1955, had a 36 percent loss rate with dozens of agents lost, while Hoxha ruled until 1985.
insights.speaker: Casey Michel
insights.so_what: A stark base rate for the catastrophic odds of amateur covert regime-change operations.

insights.claim: The real culprits were poorly trained Albanian recruits whose 'open bar talk' leaked plans, and flamboyant, hubristic CIA/MI6 officers who kept dropping agents even after teams were captured.
insights.speaker: Casey Michel
insights.so_what: Locates failure in tradecraft and hubris, not betrayal—a recurring pattern in intelligence operations.

insights.claim: The reviewer draws a direct parallel to 2026: Iran's regime looked ready to topple months ago but hung on and even strengthened, just as Hoxha outlasted Western expectations.
insights.speaker: Casey Michel
insights.so_what: A cautionary frame against pricing in near-term Iranian regime collapse.

insights.claim: CIA Director John Ratcliffe called efforts at regime change in Iran 'farcical' and was proven correct, but was overruled by an administration convinced of its path to victory.
insights.speaker: Casey Michel
insights.so_what: Shows correct intelligence assessments being ignored—policy risk driven by leadership hubris, not analysis.
